coredata.dc:title: Some neglected or rejected paths in sonochemistry – A very personal view
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:issueName: Selected papers from the 14th Meeting of the European Society of Sonochemistry, Avignon, France, June 2014
coredata.dc:creator: Mason, Timothy J.
coredata.dc:description: Sonochemistry is a very broad subject and nowadays it involves a wide range of disciplines and provides a forum for the cross-fertilization of ideas. In the early days this was not quite the situation and so a number of ideas that came forward were outside of main-line classical thinking in science. These concepts were often rejected out of hand and sometimes even ridiculed. This paper revisits some of these ideas for those of you who might be interested in the more unusual effects in sonochemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Unusual effects
Sonochemistry
Ultrasound

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: Selected papers from the 14th Meeting of the European Society of Sonochemistry, Avignon, France, June 2014

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

head.ce:title: Some neglected or rejected paths in sonochemistry – A very personal view

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sonochemistry Centre, Faculty of Health and Life Sciences, Coventry University, Coventry CV1 5FB, UK

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Health and Life Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sonochemistry is a very broad subject and nowadays it involves a wide range of disciplines and provides a forum for the cross-fertilization of ideas. In the early days this was not quite the situation and so a number of ideas that came forward were outside of main-line classical thinking in science. These concepts were often rejected out of hand and sometimes even ridiculed. This paper revisits some of these ideas for those of you who might be interested in the more unusual effects in sonochemistry.

ce:sections.ce:para: I suppose that it must be the breadth of the subject of sonochemistry that makes it an attractive field of research for such a wide range of disciplines. It follows from this that with so many disciplines involved it is bound to generate a cross-fertilization of ideas. One of the consequences is that from time to time an idea is proposed that is somewhat outside of main-line classical thinking in science. These concepts are often rejected out of hand and sometimes ridiculed. But on some occasions they are considered thoughtfully and progressed. For me it seemed quite appropriate to present a paper addressing a few of these ideas at ESS14 because I know that Jean-Louis Luche (to whose memory this Special Issue is dedicated) was interested in the unusual effects in sonochemistry.

Some of the “neglected or rejected” ideas will be the theme of this article and I will attempt to justify each one in terms of at least persuading the reader to give some more thought to them. The areas I will discuss are:
•
Ultrasonically induced conductivity change.
•
Ordering effects.
•
Ultrasonic capillary effect.
•
Sonobioreactors.
•
Acoustic spectroscopy.
•
Bubble collapse mechanisms.
•
Nuclear fusion.

ce:section.ce:para:
Observations on changes in conductivity of deionised water induced by acoustic cavitation were reported over 50years ago [1]. These changes are explicable in terms of the chemical effects of acoustic cavitation in that when an aqueous solution saturated with oxygen is sonicated hydrogen peroxide is formed. This is due to hydroxyl and hydroperoxyl recombination outside the cavitation bubble. Spin trapping experiments revealed the presence of
OH and
OOH radicals resulting from H2O and O2 homolytic cleavage inside the bubble [2,3]. However when the sonication of water is conducted in an air-equilibrated medium both nitrous and nitrite ions accompany H2O2 formation. As the reaction progresses, the rate of H2O2 production decreases and nitrite ions appear with a concentration that increases at the expense of that of nitrous ions. This observation indicates that H2O2 and NO2 − are the primary products in air-equilibrated water. NO3 − results from the oxidation of NO2 - by hydrogen peroxide [4].

While logical explanations involving sonochemical reactions are available for the above there are other changes in conductivity induced by sonication that are much less clear. For example when the electrical conductivity of nitrobenzene was measured as a function of time during sonication an increase was observed which dropped when the sonication was turned off [5]. This behaviour seems to indicate that ultrasound can induce the transient formation of some polar species or states. After switching off the irradiation the conductivity decreases and stabilises but at a final value that is slightly higher than the initial reading. The authors suggested that this was most probably due to the formation of some ionic products and they hypothesised that the intermediate states formed during sonication may be able to influence chemical reactivity.

In 1994 Mircea Vinatoru presented a paper at the COST meeting that followed the 4th meeting of the European Society of Sonochemistry held in Blankenberg, Belgium. He discussed a number of unusual observations including the suggestion that ultrasound can induce order in liquids. At the time this was a new concept for sonochemistry but since then ordering effects have been reported in the literature including the effect of an ultrasound on a normally oriented layer of nematic liquid crystal (NLC). In the absence of external action, a normally oriented NLC layer placed between crossed polaroids is opaque to the light wave that illuminates the layer along the crystal axis. The effect of an acoustic field is to tilt the crystal molecules in a way which makes the layer transparent [6].

In the 1950’s Ueda et al reported that some unusual electrical phenomena could be observed when ultrasound was applied to a capillary glass containing a solution of electrolyte [7]. They showed that at both ends of a capillary an alternating electrical field is developed in the presence of ultrasound suggesting that ultrasound was capable of causing rapid structural alignment and re-alignment of the contained solution.

Sonication-assisted aggregation through standing waves is now well-established protocol but this same phenomenon can be used to trigger molecular assembly. This is particularly important in the case of organogels and ordered structures and these have been the subject of a wide ranging tutorial review [8]. The authors emphasise the importance of the subject in connection with the formation of supramolecular assemblies, especially hydrogels, as biomaterials and vehicles for drug transport and delivery. It was concluded that it was now reasonable to consider sound waves as a means of accessing new forms of soft matter and functionalized materials.

The Ultrasonic capillary effect (UCE) was first reported in the 1960’s and is a dramatic (and anomalous) increase of the depth and velocity of penetration of a liquid into a capillary channel under some conditions of sonication. Since then the phenomenon has been little investigated and remains poorly explained. Some possible explanations have been proposed linking the enhancement of penetration of liquids into capillary channels under ultrasound with:
•
Vibrations of the capillary walls.
•
A change of the surface tension under ultrasound.
•
The development of constant pressure at the meniscus in capillaries.
•
Hydrodynamic processes at the entrance of the capillary (asymmetry of the hydrodynamic resistance for flow into and out of the capillary).
•
The mechanism of cavitation.

More recent studies by Nicolai Dezhkunov suggest that the most likely mechanism involves cavitation [9]. It was shown that with small gaps between the capillary and the emitting face of the ultrasonic transducer the thresholds for both the initiation of cavitation and for capillary rise virtually coincided. Increasing the amplitude to increase sonoluminescence also increased the ultrasonic capillary effect. The results obtained not only confirmed the direct relationship between cavitation and the ultrasonic capillary effect but also suggested the possibility of using a capillary as an indicator of the activity of acoustic cavitation.

Although the exact mechanisms for the capillary effect are not yet established the effect is clear and can be demonstrated. Also there are a number of applications of sonochemistry that involve UCE and some of these are listed below.
•
Surface cleaning is one of the oldest established commercial applications of power ultrasound. It derives its efficiency mainly through the shock waves and liquid jets generated during the collapse of acoustic cavitation bubbles. However the influence of UCE means that sonication can provide more effective cleaning and irrigation of cracks or cavities in surfaces with water or water containing low concentrations of detergent.
•
Ultrasonically assisted extraction (UAE) is now in regular use for the extraction of food additives and nutraceuticals from plants or seeds. It enhances the ability of a solvent to penetrate vegetable material ensuring that it has sufficient contact with the solvent. The use of ultrasound can improve the extraction process by increasing (a) the mass transfer between the solvent and plant material (b) the solvent penetration through crevices and capillaries within the bulk plant material and (c) the release of compounds via cell disruption induced by cavitation [10].
•
The treatment of melts in metallurgy involves a number of processes but for light metals, particularly aluminium alloys ultrasound has been used for the removal of hydrogen and fine oxides [11]. This technique is based on a sonocapillary effect and the capturing of solid inclusions in capillary channels of multilayer screen filters. The method is known as the Usfirals process and details of this and other applications of ultrasound in the treatment of light alloy melts can be found in a recently published book on the subject [12].
•
Improving the yield from failing oil wells is an important objective for industry because within existing oilfields there are many wells which have diminishing returns in terms of rate of extraction and yet may still contain large reserves of oil. These reserves are a great resource but recovery is not economic unless some form of downhole intervention can be used to release them. Currently large quantities of acids and other chemicals are used for removing blockages at the base of wells caused by limestone and dolomite but the treatment has drawbacks in terms of both cost and contamination. A method for the ultrasonic enhancement of oil recovery has been developed which involves lowering a source of power ultrasound to the bottom of the well either for a short treatment before it is removed or as a permanent placement for intermittent use. Cavitation and shockwaves induced by the powerful ultrasonic transducer clears the debris that is clogging surrounding oil-bearing strata and improves the penetration of downhole chemicals and oil through the cracks and capillaries. Overall such treatment can increase productivity up to nearly 3-fold [13].
•
The clean-up of polluted soil is an important process in the rehabilitation of industrial sites or the reclamation of polluted land and power ultrasound can be used for the removal of both chemical and biological contamination. There are two basic mechanisms for acoustically enhanced soil cleaning that have been suggested (a) an increase in the abrasion of suspended soil in slurries leading to the removal of contaminated material from the surface of particles and (b) an improvement in leaching out of more deeply entrenched materials by greater solvent penetration through capillaries and cracks [14]. The latter factor is also the reason for improved leaching of metals from ores [15].
•
The visualisation of surface cracks is traditionally carried out by wetting the surface, allowing moisture to enter fine cracks and after wiping the specimen dust it with powder. Moisture will remain in the cracks and the powder will collect in those regions where it absorbs the moisture. When the excess powder is blown away the cracks are revealed by the presence of adherent wet powder. The process relies on the penetration of water into the cracks and this can be improved by ultrasonically vibrating the surface in the first stage of wetting giving much better solvent penetration [16].

The use of sonication in microbiology is normally associated with the control or killing of cells where power ultrasound is used to disrupt bacteria cells through cavitation e.g. for the release of cell contents in the microbiology laboratory. It has also been used in environmental protection [17] and food sterilisation [18]. However there is another application of ultrasound at much lowers, sometimes at higher frequencies, where sonication can lead to the enhancement of cell activity. Lower power ultrasound i.e. at a power less than that required for cell disruption can produce several effects in microbiology.
•
The break-up of cell clumps which separates the cells into individual entities and removes the “shielding” from cells contained within clumps thus making more individual cells active.
•
Cell wall weakening which occurs only while the ultrasound is applied will enable faster transfer of materials into and out of the cell across the outer membrane.
•
Induced microstreaming within the cell that gives better mass transfer and movement within the bacterial cells themselves.

An example of the enhancement of cell activity in organic synthesis was published in nearly 30years ago and related to the enhanced oxidation of cholesterol using Rhodococcus erythropolis [19]. Using an optimised sonication regime of 20kHz, 2.2W/cm2, pulsed at 5 sec every minute at 26°C the cell structure was maintained and metabolic activity enhanced to give a faster rate and 40% increase in yield. There have been a considerable number of examples of this type of enhancement which have been reviewed [20,21] but despite this and up to now it does not seem that sonochemical enhancement has been taken up as a method of choice.

Another area of laboratory research has been the sonochemical activation of immobilised enzymes where ultrasound appears to be particularly useful in increasing mass transport of substrate to the enzyme. In the same way as is the case with enhanced microbial activity these studies also appear to have been neglected. Early examples include the use of α-chymotrypsin (on agarose gel) and casein as substrate where a twofold increase in activity was observed at 20kHz [22]. Here the origin of the enhancement was thought to be associated with increased penetration of the casein into the support gel induced by cavitational effects close to the surface. In contrast an increase in the activity of s-amylase (on porous polystyrene) was produced on irradiation at the much higher frequency of 7MHz [23]. This is a very significant result since at this high frequency cavitation cannot occur and so, in this case, the increased activity is thought to be associated with the promotion of the diffusion processes predominately within the Nernst diffusion layer of the carrier beads.

The attenuation of ultrasound has traditionally been of interest in materials characterisation and has a range of applications in chemical analysis. One area is that involving the determination of solvent structure e.g. in the determination of water alcohol structures. As ethanol is added to water, the water acts as host to the alcohol molecules and the structural order of the water increases until a critical region of maximum structure is reached (0.3mol fraction of alcohol). The attenuation of sound by these different mixtures is found to depend upon the concentration i.e. degree of water ethanol interaction and the relationship is a curve which forms a maximum in attenuation at around 0.3 mole fraction of ethanol i.e. the point of maximum structure [24]. According to Franks and Ives this structural enhancement derives from the replacement of some water-water H-bonds by strong hydrogen bonding between water and the hydroxyl groups of the alcohol [25,26]. With increasing concentration of ethanol, beyond the point of maximum structure the water can no longer accommodate the alcohol molecules and depolymerisation of the three dimensional structure of water occurs.

A much less common way in which the attenuation of ultrasound can be used to investigate solvent structures involves the examination of a liquid mixture of fixed composition over a range of different ultrasound frequencies. This is known as acoustic spectroscopy and in its early years it was used as a method of observing the structure of proteins in water [27]. It has also been used to determine the molecular relaxation properties of a number of monosaccharides in aqueous solution have been studied using broad band ultrasonic spectrometry in the frequency range 0.2–2000MHz [28]. The results revealed ultrasonic excess absorption with relaxation characteristics near 80MHz for glucose and the methyl glucosides of d-glucose and d-mannose, but no relaxation process was detected for the other monosaccharides in the same frequency range. From structural aspects it was deduced that the most likely process causing the observed relaxation is the rotation of the exocyclic –CH2OH group, placing rotational isomerization on the nanosecond time scale.

In the early days of sonochemistry there was much discussion centred around what actually happened inside of the collapsing bubble and what gave rise to sonoluminescence and sonochemical reactions. Some of the work was based upon mathematical modelling and calculations of the possible temperatures generated during the collapse but other work speculated upon what actually was the state of matter within the bubble. All of the scientists involved agreed that there were shock waves and jets generated in the bulk medium since this was evidenced by both physical effects and photographic evidence [29,30].

At that time in the development of sonochemistry there were a lot of discussions (sometimes heated) about what was actually happening to the material inside of the collapsing bubble. Ideas were proposed involving electrical or corona discharges, plasma formation, supercritical fluid formation and chemical reactions resulting from liquid droplets injected inside the bubble.

Various electrical theories have been developed the earliest of which dates back to the work of Frenkel who suggested that electrical charges could be built up on opposite faces of a cavitation bubble as it is formed [31]. An alternative explanation was offered some years later based upon the production of an electric discharge during bubble collapse [32]. According to this theory the bubble undergoes pulsations then fragmentation during which intense electrical fields estimated to be at least 1011 Vm−1 are generated. Subsequently an idea was put forward at the Third Meeting of the European Society of Sonochemistry Figuera da Foz, Portugal, in 1993 in which electrified sprays of the surrounding liquid were thought to be injected into the cavity [33]. As a result partial ionisation of the bubble content could be produced and analogies with cold plasma chemistry were recorded. At around that time Jean Louis Luche and I wrote the following about these ideas in a chapter of a book compiled from the proceedings of the current EU COST programme “It is of course quite possible that both interpretations are correct but that the true situation is a hybrid which leans towards one or the other depending upon the precise reaction conditions” [34].

A plasma theory was proposed by Lepoint et al. [35] which assumed that the cavitational collapse creates a microplasma inside the collapsing bubble highly charged with energy. Recent spectroscopic studies of single bubble (SBSL) and multibubble (MBSL) sonoluminescence appear to support this by suggesting that the origin of extreme intrabubble conditions is related to nonequilibrium plasma formation inside the collapsing bubbles [36].

Other research has led to the idea that during the sonication of water the cavitation bubbles can generate supercritical water which Hoffmann invoked as a possible contributor to the hydrolysis of 4-nitrophenyl acetate [37]. Although von Sonntag argued that any (transient) supercritical water layer could not be involved [38].

The most popular and widely accepted theory is the so-called hot-spot theory the terminology for which can be traced back to 1956 [39]. According to this the collapse of an acoustic cavitation bubble develops such great intensities of pressure and temperature that it can be considered as a tiny “hotspot” of energy generating extremes of heat and pressure for molecules contained in the bubble.

In a sonicated liquid there are many high energy collapsing cavitation bubbles or hotspots and that this energy can be amplified further when the overpressure on the fluid is increased. For many years it has been conjectured that under such high energy conditions it might be possible to induce nuclear fusion of atoms contained in the collapsing bubble. If this could be done then the result would mean a new and clean energy source for the world and the potential for this would be dramatic. In essence it is the “holy grail” of energy generation and explains why so many university and government groups throughout the world have been diligently searching for ways of producing nuclear fusion.

The approaches to man-made nuclear fusion have mainly centred on plasma technology but in 2002 a piece of work published by Taleyarkahan et al in which he claimed to have observed nuclear fusion through sonication caused quite a stir in the scientific community [40]. The experiment involved the firing of neutron pulses into sonicated deuteroacetone under pressure in order to generate cavitation bubbles. He claimed to have observed statistically significant traces of extra neutrons and the formation of radioactive isotope tritium via the fusion reaction:
D + D
→
H or T + neuron

Unfortunately this work came under severe criticism over his claim that he had observed an excess neutrons generated by fusion. No other laboratories were able to reproduce the work and it fell into disrepute. It has not however deterred laboratories from continuing the search for nuclear fusion energy via sonochemistry but no further claims have appeared in the literature to date.

I would like to think that Jean-Louis Luche would have given serious consideration to all of these neglected or rejected paths and other strange or inexplicable aspects of sonochemistry. Certainly I do not believe that we as sonochemists, whatever our particular scientific leaning, should discount any new ideas in our subject until they have been thoroughly tried and tested.
